webinar registration page. This allows us to warm them up before we're asking them to register for a webinar. As you're advertising, it's easy to measure cost per registrant or even cost per attendee. But I recommend that that is not what you're focusing on because it's going to be high. When you see those numbers, you're going to be saying, I'm spending $10 per person who registers for my webinar or I'm spending $17 per person who actually attends my webinar. Instead, I recommend you measure cost per conversion because that's really the only thing that matters in the end is how much did it cost for each person who bought your particular product sold on your webinar. That's what I recommend your measure. Measure the other ones, but don't freak out because the numbers are going to be high. The only thing that matters is, did you pay less than you made for the actual sales that come from your webinar? Now, let's talk about a targeting piece here for a moment. Lookalike audiences. Lookalike audiences are people who act like an audience that you already have. This is a common term used in Facebook and other ad platforms. So lookalike audiences that you may want to use to audiences that you may want to create a lookalike audience from include buyers of your product from previous webinars. So this is in priority order. The first one is ideally if you've run this webinar a bunch and people have come to your webinar, have bought your product on your webinar and you've got a large enough number of those people over 200, ideally over a thousand people, then that's the group of people that you're building a lookalike based upon. Unfortunately, most of us won't have that until we've run the webinar a number of times. And so the next priority is buyers of your product. So you take the number of buyers of your product, you take that group and you upload it into Facebook and you create a lookalike audience based upon them. Now, you may not have over 200 or 1000 people who bought your product yet.
